counterpoints.text: STRONGEST COUNTER TO MY OWN VIEW: granularity pushes correct sequencing onto every adapter author, and a leaky orchestrator can violate runtime invariants the runtime could have enforced itself. A composed round-step implemented as a pure-local reducer over an already-written collect-result.json (collect-result -> append-message* -> finalize-round) that never crosses the host seam would NOT touch host-agnosticism, COULD emit the same per-step events internally, and would centralize the ordering invariants so N adapters cannot get them wrong.

Guarding my blind spot: I may conflate call-count with complexity. Six commands per round is not itself a defect; adapter-author ergonomics are a real cost. Granularity must win on auditability/resume semantics, not on 'fewer commands feels impure.'

result.currentPosition: Keep granular transport/WAL primitives; do not ship a composed round-step as a runtime command. Composition, if any, belongs in the adapter or as a strictly post-collect runtime reducer that never crosses the host seam.
result.position: Keep the granular transport/WAL primitives. swarm-discussion-runtime must NOT ship a single composed round-step command. A round-step inherently straddles the host-execution seam (transport-init hands a spawn-order across to the host, which performs the spawn/wait the runtime cannot perform), so any 'composed' command is either a lie about atomicity or a wrapper that silently re-enters the host. Composition belongs to the adapter/orchestrator layer, not the runtime contract.
result.previousPosition: Keep granular transport/WAL primitives; do not ship a composed round-step as a runtime command. Composition, if any, belongs in the adapter or as a strictly post-collect runtime reducer that never crosses the host seam.

proposals.text: Keep the five-verb spine granular: transport-init / transport-append-batch / transport-collect on the host seam, and append-message / checkpoint / finalize-round on the WAL. Each stays single-responsibility, individually fsync-durable, and individually replayable from resume_plan state.
If round-step ergonomics are wanted, ship the composition as an adapter-level orchestration recipe, NOT a runtime subcommand.
Make resume_plan the single source of 'what to run next' so callers never need a composed command to know the next step.
Adopt an explicit invariant: no runtime subcommand may invoke the host transport. transport-init's contract is 'write artifacts and stop at the seam.'

questions.text: Can the proposed round-step be cleanly bisected at the host seam — a post-collect reducer that provably never invokes the host?
If a composed round-step is added, what single on-disk marker would resume_plan read to distinguish 'composed-step in progress' from a bare partial?
Would a composed command still emit each underlying event individually, or collapse them? If collapsed, audit resolution drops.

result.reasoning: Three invariants from the actual code drive this. (1) AUDITABILITY: every primitive emits its own fsync'd event to events.jsonl and an atomic-rename artifact; the audit trail's resolution equals the command boundary. A composed round-step collapses transport-init -> host-spawn -> append-batch -> collect -> append-message(*N) -> finalize-round into one event horizon; when it fails mid-way you can no longer read events.jsonl and say which sub-step committed. (2) RESUMABILITY AFTER A REAP: resume_plan reconstructs intent purely from on-disk seq state and works only because each primitive leaves a well-defined, individually-durable on-disk state; a composed command has intermediate states resume_plan cannot name. (3) HOST-AGNOSTIC SEAM: the runtime is host-agnostic precisely because transport-init STOPS at writing artifacts; the host owns spawn/wait. A composed round-step would either hard-code host transport (breaking host-agnosticism) or shell back to the adapter, at which point it is an orchestrator script, not a runtime command.
result.shiftReason: first declaration, no peer input yet

counterpoints.claim: Strongest counter to my own view
counterpoints.detail: Granular primitives are intentional precisely because per-step WAL records and per-phase resume points are the substrate of crash recovery and post-hoc audit; a composed command, even a careful one, tempts implementers toward atomic-or-nothing semantics that erase the transport-sent/WAL-uncommitted boundary an auditor and a resuming adapter both need. The call-count win is paid once at integration time, but the auditability/resumability cost is paid forever by operators debugging divergent replays. If the composite cannot provably emit identical records and identical resume points, granular primitives are the correct default and my preference for fewer calls is actively harmful.

result.currentPosition: Ship a composed round-step command as the default integration surface, while keeping the granular transport/WAL primitives exported and supported underneath it.
result.position: Ship a composed round-step command as the default integration surface, while keeping the granular transport/WAL primitives exported and supported underneath it.
result.previousPosition: Ship a composed round-step command as the default integration surface, while keeping the granular transport/WAL primitives exported and supported underneath it.

proposals.detail: Add a single round_step that internally calls the existing primitives in the runtime-owned correct order; keep all granular primitives public. Adapters reach for the composite by default; only those needing custom interleaving drop to primitives.
proposals.title: Composite as the default, primitives as the escape hatch

proposals.detail: Mandate round_step write the exact same per-primitive WAL records a hand-rolled sequence would. Auditability must be a property of the record stream, not of which command was called.
proposals.title: Identical WAL emission under the composite

proposals.detail: round_step accepts and returns a step-phase cursor so a crash mid-step resumes at the last committed boundary instead of replaying or losing the round.
proposals.title: Resumable step cursor

proposals.detail: The composite returns which internal phase failed (transport vs WAL) plus the resume cursor, so failure granularity survives the wrapper.
proposals.title: Structured per-phase failure return

proposals.detail: One runtime-side test asserting the order and emitted WAL record set, replacing N untested re-implementations across adapters.
proposals.title: Conformance test pinning order and records

questions.q: Can round_step emit byte-identical WAL records and expose the same resume boundaries as the hand-rolled sequence, or does compositing force a coarser atomic unit?
What fraction of real adapters need custom primitive interleaving versus the standard order — is the composite covering the 95% case or just the demo path?
Does the host-agnostic constraint permit the composite to own the ordering contract, or does any host need to inject behavior between transport and WAL?

result.reasoning: As the person who writes and maintains host adapters, I count integration cost in call sites, not elegance. A round today forces an adapter author to orchestrate transport send, transport receive, WAL append, and WAL commit in the right order with error handling on each seam — four-plus primitives whose ordering contract lives in prose and tribal knowledge. Every adapter re-implements that choreography, every adapter drifts, and the bugs are the boring kind (WAL appended before transport confirmed, commit skipped on a partial send) no reviewer catches until a replay diverges. A composed round_step collapses that to one call with one well-defined failure return, moving the ordering contract from prose into the runtime where it is tested once instead of N times across hosts. 'Command granularity is intentional today' is a known fact, but granularity that every consumer must reassemble identically is a design smell — the runtime leaking its internal state machine onto host authors. The honest cost is real: a naive atomic-or-nothing round_step hurts resumability and per-step auditability — you lose the per-primitive WAL records that let an auditor reconstruct exactly where a round failed. The resolution is composite-on-top-of-primitives: round_step must emit the same discrete WAL records and expose a resumable step cursor, so audit trail and resume points are identical whether you call the composite or hand-roll. If it cannot preserve that, ship it as a tested reference helper rather than the canonical command.
